coredata.dc:title: Table tennis bat cypress baseplate derived fluorescence carbon dots: In vivo bioimaging applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xing
Zhang, Ying
Wu, Yueting
Li, Li
Liu, Yufei
Nguyen, Tat Thang
Du, Chunhua
Zhou, Tongxi
Wei, Yaru
Du, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Inspired by sports, this is the first study where waste table tennis bat cypress baseplates have been used as a carbon source in order to sustainably synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs). The prepared FCDs were suitable for bioimaging of the C2C12 muscle myoblasts, as well as in vivo bioimaging. The average particle size of the amorphous FCDs was ∼7.45 nm, whereas nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the carbon structure were observed. The fabricated FCDs exhibit excellent water solubility, biocompatibility and photoluminescent (PL) properties, while the maximum PL intensity was found at 401 nm on the excitation wavelength at 334 nm with a fluorescence lifetime of 6.43 ns. On top of that, the FCDs displayed a wavelength-dependent emission over the 315–355 nm excitation window with an emission range of 385–405 nm, resulting in blue fluorescence emission. Furthermore, the synthesis mechanism of the FCDs was thoroughly explored. These attractive results indicate that sporting goods waste can also be an ideal alternative raw material for the development of fluorescence carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Table tennis bat cypress baseplate
Fluorescent carbon dots
C2C12 muscle myoblast
In vivo bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of FCDs from table tennis bat cypress baseplates
Morphological and structural analysis of FCDs
Cellular imaging and in vivo imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) SEM images and (d) EDS spectra of the table tennis bat cypress baseplate powder.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the FCDs and (c) their diameter distributions.
(a) XRD and (b) FT-IR spectra of the raw material and FCDs.
(a) XPS survey spectra of the raw material and FCDs; High-resolution XPS (b) C 1s spectrum, (c) O 1s spectrum and (d) N 1s spectrum of the FCDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum (λex = 334 nm) of FCDs; (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra and (c) CIE chromaticity diagram with different excitation spectra of FCDs; (d) Fluorescence decay curves (λex = 334 nm) of FCDs.

Fluorescence images of C2C12 cells after 24 h of incubation with FCDs: (a, c) cells incubated with water as control; (b, d) Cells incubated with FCDs; The images from (a, b) TRITC (red) channel and (c, d) FITC (green) channel, respectively.

(a) In vivo fluorescence imaging of nude mice after intravenous injection of FCDs (fluorescence images were acquired at 0, 10, 30, 60, and 120 min); (b) Representative fluorescence images of the dissected organs of a mouse after 2 h intravenous injection of FCDs. For all the images, the employed excitation wavelength was 334 nm.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of FCDs from table tennis bat cypress baseplates, and their imaging of C2C12 muscle myoblasts for in vivo bioimaging.
Schematic diagram of the hydrothermal synthesis of FCDs from table tennis bat cypress baseplates.
Element analysis of table tennis bat cypress baseplate.

head.ce:title: Table tennis bat cypress baseplate derived fluorescence carbon dots: In vivo bioimaging applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Wood Industry and Interior Design

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Wood Industry and Interior Design, Vietnam National University of Forestry, Xuan Mai, Hanoi 13417, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by sports, this is the first study where waste table tennis bat cypress baseplates have been used as a carbon source in order to sustainably synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs). The prepared FCDs were suitable for bioimaging of the C2C12 muscle myoblasts, as well as in vivo bioimaging. The average particle size of the amorphous FCDs was ∼7.45 nm, whereas nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the carbon structure were observed. The fabricated FCDs exhibit excellent water solubility, biocompatibility and photoluminescent (PL) properties, while the maximum PL intensity was found at 401 nm on the excitation wavelength at 334 nm with a fluorescence lifetime of 6.43 ns. On top of that, the FCDs displayed a wavelength-dependent emission over the 315–355 nm excitation window with an emission range of 385–405 nm, resulting in blue fluorescence emission. Furthermore, the synthesis mechanism of the FCDs was thoroughly explored. These attractive results indicate that sporting goods waste can also be an ideal alternative raw material for the development of fluorescence carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are considered as a kind of fluorescent nanodots, while their carbon cores and the related surface ligands usually exhibit unique optical properties [1–4]. Compared with the conventional expensive and toxic fluorescent dyes, CDs possess the significant characteristics of biocompatibility, non-toxicity, adjustable photoluminescence distribution, excellent stability, good water solubility, and easy functionalization process. Therefore, CDs have been used as fluorescent probes, which are widely employed in the fields of bioimaging, drug carriers and sensing [5–10]. As far as the bioimaging aspect is concerned, several reports have successfully demonstrated the synthesis of CDs by enforcing different technologies and the subsequent application for imaging HeLa cells, A549 cells, L02 cells, and macrophage cells. While for future applications, the vivo bioimaging technique is considered of outmost importance.

Generally, the carbon source for the fabrication of CDs originates from chemicals. In recent years, due to enhanced economic and environmental needs, biomass materials with high carbon content and abundant heteroatoms stand out, which have the ability to become renewable, low-cost, non-toxic and environmentally friendly natural raw materials [1,11,12]. Interestingly, such types of materials can also produce CDs on a large scale. Along these lines, a variety of biomass-based CDs has been developed by the scientific community [13,14]. For instance, the carbon dots (CS-CQDs) with in vivo imaging advantages prepared from coffee bean shells have the ability to gather at the tumor site of nude mice and do not interfere with their internal organs [15]. Furthermore, the nude mice were still alive 6 days after the injection process. In addition, lemon juice-derived carbon dots (CQDs) can be also used as near-infrared fluorescent probes for in vitro or in vivo biological imaging [16]. They display effective penetration in mouse skin and tissues with stronger photoluminescence than the background signals. Several studies have shown that the natural wood in biomass is mainly composed of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin [17–19]. Among them, the high carbon and the polysaccharide contents in cellulose and hemicellulose, as well as the unique self-association and fluorescence emission characteristics of lignin, indicate that wood possesses great potential in the preparation of the fluorescent carbon dots. For instance, the lignin-based carbon dots (L-CDs) prepared from cellulolytic enzyme lignin can emit fluorescence signals in the cytoplasm of HeLa cells under single-photon and two-photon excitation [20].

The sporting goods that are manufactured with natural wood and other materials by different processes include table tennis bat, cue, and basketball stands, and so on. Consequently, the remained wood waste during the production of these sporting goods can be regarded as a potential carbon source. However, most of the employed treatment methods are landfill or incineration, which not only results in severe material waste but also damages the ecological environment. Therefore, in this study, low-cost fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) were obtained from the table tennis bat cypress baseplates easily by enforcing a facile hydrothermal method, and investigating their bioimaging potential application in muscle myoblast C2C12 and in vivo (Scheme 1 ). In addition, the underlying synthesis mechanism of FCDs is thoroughly analyzed.

The table tennis bat cypress baseplates (10 ± 1 mm) were the defective products discarded by the factory. The employed reagents including ethyl alcohol and ethylenediamine were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Regent Company and used without enforcing any further purification. Deionized water was used in all synthesis procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of FCDs from table tennis bat cypress baseplates
ce:section.ce:para: The employed table tennis bat cypress baseplates were grinded into scraps with a carpenter's plane, which were further grounded into powder by a small high-speed grinder. Then the powder was placed on an 80-mesh sieve. Afterward, the deionized water was added to the powder sample after sieving was washed with an ultrasonic cleaner, and finally dried as a raw material for use. The raw material (1 g) was then mixed with deionized water (20 mL), while 100 μL ethylenediamine (EDA) was added. Then, it was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (volume 100 mL) by the application of a hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 12 h. The reacted solution was filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm) in order to remove the solid impurities, and finally, the transparent brown-yellow solution (FCDs) was collected. The preparation of the FCDs nanocrystals is as follows: a part of the FCDs solution was taken out and freeze-dried in a vacuum for 36 h for obtaining a solid brown powder product. These products were used for further characterization and application. Finally, the aqueous solution of FCDs nanocrystals was stored at 5 °C before use.

The morphology of the samples was observed by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM). As far as the elemental analysis of the samples is concerned, it was carried out by employing an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDAX). In addition, the microstructures were determined by performing (high-resolution) transmission electron microscope (TEM/HRTEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL) measurements. The crystal structure was obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis by using an X-ray diffractometer (BRUKERD8-ADVANCE, Germany) with an operating energy of 40 KV/100 mA. Furthermore, the FTIR spectra were analyzed by using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, EQUNOX-55, Germany BRUKER) within the operating frequency range of 450–4000 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded by employing an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ThermoFisher K-Alpha, Shenzhen Junhuiteng Technology Co., Ltd, China). For the UV–vis absorption spectra, a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 5000) was used, with a range from 200 to 900 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed by employing a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000). Finally, the time decaying data was detected by using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000), while the lifetime curves were calculated and fitted from the respective time decaying data.

The prepared FCDs were measured for cell imaging tests on C2C12 mouse muscle myoblasts (ATCC® CRL-1772™ via Sigma, Australia) in order to evaluate the application potential of FCDs, as far as the cell bioimaging method is concerned. Besides, the C2C12 cells were stored at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The cells were also seeded on coverslips pretreated with poly-l-lysine. After 24 h, the FCDs were added to the medium at a volume ratio of 1:2 (FCDs: medium) and incubated for a certain period. The cells in the control experiment were treated with an equal volume of sterile water. For the cell imaging process, all coverslips with cells were fixed with 4% formalin for 15 min, washed with PBS, and then mounted on glass slides with/or without DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole). The cells were observed by employing a fluorescence microscope (Nikon-Tish-A1R-MP, Melville, USA) at a 20× setting. All the acquired images were converted to the tagged information file format, and processed by using the Adobe Photoshop program (Photoshop CC 2015, Adobe, San Jose, CA, USA).

All the described animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the regulations of Nanjing Jiangning Qinglongshan Animal Center. In vivo imaging was performed by using PE IVIS Lumina XR small animal imaging system from PerkinElmer, USA. Additionally, the nude mice were injected with FCDs solution (20 μL) through the tail vein, and the images were recorded before and at 10, 30, 60, and 120 min after the injection. The mice were euthanized, and the main tissues and organs were dissected 2 h after the injection in order to obtain the fluorescence images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural analysis of FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of FCDs were also observed by TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. 2 ). The acquired TEM image (Fig. 2a) divulges that the FCDs are spherically distributed with good dispersion and no obvious aggregation. Moreover, as can be ascertained from Fig. 2b where the HRTEM image of the FCDs is presented, there are slight lattices inside the FCDs with a lattice plane spacing of ∼0.22 nm, which correspond to the (100) diffraction facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [1,24,25]. This result indicates that FCDs exhibits graphite-like carbon cores. According to the histogram of the frequency distribution of the FCDs diameter (Fig. 2c), an average particle size of 7.45 nm is recorded.

The crystal structures of both FCDs and the raw materials were analyzed by the XRD technique. As it is revealed in Fig. 3 a, by analyzing the XRD pattern of the raw material, several diffraction peaks appearing at 2θ = ∼15.7° and 22.4°, which correspond to the type Ⅰ crystal lattice of the cellulose [19,26–28]. It is interesting to notice that the XRD pattern of FCDs discloses a broad diffraction peak centered at ∼22.1° [19,29,30], which is similar to the graphite-like carbon materials and can be well described by considering the Scherrer formula:
(1)
D =
K λ
β
( θ )
cos
( θ )
where D, K, β, θ, and λ are the average crystallite size, constant, peak width of the physical broadening of sample, Bragg angle of the diffraction peak, and wavelength of the diffraction line, respectively. As a result, according to Eq. (1), the interplanar spacing of the carbon cores of FCDs can be calculated as ∼0.39 nm, which is consistent with the (002) planes of the graphitic carbon [19], except that it is slightly larger than that of graphitic carbon (0.34 nm). This effect could be ascribed to the incorporation of abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups in the FCDs during the hydrothermal treatment [31], increasing thus their amorphous nature.

The FT-IR spectra of FCDs and raw materials are revealed in Fig. 3b. By considering the FT-IR spectra of the two samples, the –OH stretching vibration mode appears between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 [32]. Additionally, the characteristic absorption bands that correspond to cellulose can be detected in the FT-IR spectra of the raw materials. More specifically, the bands at 1100 and 1045 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of the C–O bond at the secondary hydroxyl group at the C2, C3, whereas the primary hydroxyl group at the C6 position on the glucose ring of cellulose [19]. Moreover, the bands at 2899, 1434, and 896 cm−1 correspond to C–H stretching vibration [33], –CH2 in-plane bending vibration, and vibration of the anomeric carbon (C1), respectively. The anomeric carbon (C1) is located on the glucose rings of the cellulose unit. In addition, the characteristic absorption band, which is found at 1741 cm−1 corresponds to CO derived from aldehyde and carboxyl [34,35]. Therefore, this effect demonstrates the main chemical components of the table tennis bat cypress baseplates is cellulose [19], which is consistent with the acquired XRD results.

It is interesting to notice that the absorption band of FCDs, which is located at 1342 cm−1 is assigned to the C–N stretching vibration of aromatic primary amine [36,37], while the absorption bands at 1546 cm−1, 1397 cm−1, 1016 cm−1 and 646 cm−1 can be attributed to the manifestation of unsaturated CC bonds, heterocyclic N–(C)3, C–O–C stretching vibration [38–40], as well as the aromatic C–H out-of-plane stretching vibration of the aromatic compound molecule, respectively. As a result, we can draw the conclusion that after the enforcement of the hydrothermal treatment of cellulose and EDA, FCDs with nitrogen-doped carbonized cores and a large number of amino groups on the surface are formed [31,41,42]. In addition, in the IR spectra of FCDs, the CO band of aldehyde and carboxyl from the raw material disappeared, while the CO absorption band of the amide group emerged at 1676 cm−1.

Furthermore, the elemental composition and the surface chemical structure of both the raw materials and FCDs are characterized by XPS (Fig. 4 ). As it is disclosed in Fig. 4a, only the C 1s (285.6 eV) and O 1s (533.5 eV) peaks can be detected in the XPS spectrum of the raw material, while in the respective XPS spectrum of FCDs, except for C 1s (285.6 eV) and O 1s (530.4 eV) peaks, the peak of N 1s (398.6 eV) is also found. These results indicate that nitrogen has been successfully introduced with the FCDs configuration during the hydrothermal reaction [19]. On top of that, as it is displayed in Fig. 4b, the C 1s spectrum of FCDs reveals the existence of five different peaks at 284.6, 286.0, 287.1, 287.8 and 285.2 eV, which can be assigned to the following bonds: C–C/CC, C–N, C–O–C, C–CO, and O–CO/CN, respectively. As far as the O 1s spectrum of FCDs is concerned (Fig. 4c), it can be fitted to four peaks of 530.8, 531.5, 532.4 and 533.3 eV, which correspond to the following bond configurations CO, C–OH, C–O–C and H2O–CO respectively. Hence, it can be concluded that FCDs possess abundant polar groups, including hydroxyl, alkyl and carboxyl groups, which is consistent with the above-mentioned XRD analysis. The existence of these functional groups in FCDs is the underlying reason for their improved dispersion/dissolution properties in water. In addition, the N 1s spectrum of FCDs (Fig. 4d) divulges four peaks at 399.1, 399.5, 400.2, and 401.2 eV, which can be assigned to CN–C, C–N–C, N–(C)3/H–N–(C)2, and N–H, respectively. Therefore, it can be established that nitrogen is not only doped within the graphite-like core of FCDs, but also forms amino groups on the surface of FCDs, which is consistent with the outcomes of the FT-IR analysis.

The schematic diagram of the underlying synthesis mechanism of FCDs is highlighted in Scheme 2 . We have to underline that the chemical composition of the raw material possesses numerous cellulose compounds. During the hydrothermal process, initially, a part of the amorphous regions of the cellulose, which is composed of randomly distributed molecular chainsis, are hydrolyzed into the carboxylic and aldehyde derivatives of monosaccharides and polysaccharides (structural formulas 1, 2 and 3) [19,43,44]. These polysaccharide derivatives are consequently further hydrolyzed into monomers. Secondly, the monosaccharide derivatives undergo a dehydration reaction in order to form a variety of hydroxymethylfurfural and its derivatives (structural formulas 4, 5 and 6) [19,43,44]. As EDA is introduced into the reaction system under polymerization conditions, the initial structure of the nitrogen-doped core of FCDs is formed (structural formula 7). Finally, as the hydrothermal time and processing temperature increase, the intermediate products are subjected to the aromatization and condensation reactions (including aldol condensation, esterification, and Schiff base condensation), producing thus the fluorescent FCDs that is composed of N, O co-doped cores (these cores are rich in amide, amino, hydroxyl, aldehyde, ether, and ester groups).

The extracted UV–vis absorption curve of FCDs is presented in Fig. 5 . More specifically, two absorption peaks appear at 280 and 345 nm in the UV region, which are closely related to the typical π→π* transition band of the aromatic (CC) sp2 hybrid orbitals, as well as and n→π* transition band of the (CO) sp3 hybrid orbitals (functional group on the surface of FCDs), respectively [45–47].

Fig. 6 a depicts the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum of the FCDs aqueous solution under the maximum excitation wavelength of 344 nm, indicating that the maximum emission is concentrated around 401 nm (the inset of Fig. 6a, a fluorescence image of FCDs excited under 365 nm UV light). Moreover, the PL emission spectra of the FCDs aqueous solution under the application of different excitation wavelengths are revealed in Fig. 6b. It is interesting to notice that as the excitation wavelength increases from 315 nm to 355 nm, the extracted PL emission peak of FCDs red-shifts to longer wavelengths from 385 nm to 405 nm. Furthermore, the increase of the fluorescence intensity of FCDs is accompanied by the enhancement of the excitation wavelength, which gradually decreases when it exceeds the value of 340 nm. Therefore, this effect indicates that FCDs exhibit a wavelength-dependent emission pattern. The CIE (chromaticity diagram) coordinates also demonstrate the fluorescence color under the enforcement of different excitations, which can be observed that the FCDs aqueous solution produces blue emission (Fig. 6c). Although the photoluminescence mechanism of quantum dots is not well understood at present, the acquired photoluminescence of FCDs may be attributed to their aggregation in the aqueous solution. Thus, the fluorescence emission of carbon nuclei is promoted, which may be mainly related to the amide moieties and carbonyl moieties [48]. In addition, the fluorescence decay curve of FCDs (Fig. 6d) is well fitted by a double exponential formula, and the fluorescence lifetime is calculated as follows:
(2) τ = (A1τ1 2+A2τ2 2)/(A1τ1+A2τ2)

As a result, according to Eq. (2), the fluorescence lifetime of FCDs is 6.43 ns, which is within the average range of both the organic dyes and quantum dots. These results indicate that FCDs possess the respective properties of organic dyes and quantum dots [49,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular imaging and in vivo imaging

ce:section.ce:para: C2C12 is a mice muscle myoblast cell line, which is widely used for studying muscle formation in vitro [51]. The employed C2C12 cells were incubated with the prepared FCDs. After the elapse of 24 h of incubation, the potential application of FCDs in cell imaging was investigated. As can be ascertained from Fig. 7 , compared with the control cells (Fig. 7a and c), the C2C12 cells clearly internalized FCDs, whereas a high fluorescence intensity can be observed in both green and red filters (Fig. 7b and d). More importantly, no significant cell death was detected in this experiment, which renders FCDs an important candidate for bioimaging applications [11,41]. Therefore, FCDs were selected for the following in vivo imaging experiments.

Encouraged by the cell imaging experiments, in vivo bioimaging experiments of FCDs were subsequently carried out (Fig. 8 ). More specifically, nude mice were injected with FCD nanocrystal solution through the tail vein and then the fluorescence signals of the various parts of the mice were detected. Apparently, no fluorescence signal could be detected before the injection process (Figs. 8a and 0 min). In contrast, 10, 30 and 60 min after injection, obvious fluorescent signals could be recorded in the middle part of the mice (Fig. 8a, 10, 30 and 60 min). Finally, by injecting after 2 h, a fluorescent signal was detected at the rear end of the mice (Figs. 8a and 120 min). These results clearly indicated that the FCDs were cycled in the whole body [25]. Furthermore, 2 h after injection, the mice were euthanized and dissected in order to investigate the distribution of FCDs, which was displayed in Fig. 8b. The acquired fluorescence signals could be observed in the liver, stomach, kidney and intestines, signifying that these organs had uptake FCDs. It is interesting to notice that most fluorescent FCDs were aggregated in the large intestine. Therefore, based on the observed fluorescent signal distribution of FCDs in organs, the whole process can be proposed as follows: firstly, FCDs moved into the stomach and then absorbed by the liver, nextly, FCDs entered the small intestine through the enterohepatic circulation. However, FCDs did not remain in the small intestine, but continued their journey into the large intestine. Finally, FCDs were accumulated in the large intestine and divulged the strongest fluorescence emission [25]. During the circulation of the FCDs, both the heart and lungs of the mice were not disturbed.

Novel FCDs were easily acquired from the table tennis bat cypress baseplates, which are a neglected waste material. By enforcing a hydrothermal reaction, the FCDs were synthesized through the hydrolysis, dehydration, polymerization process with EDA, as well as aromatization and condensation of cellulose. The FCDs possess a nearly spherical morphology with an average diameter of ∼7.45 nm and exhibit an acceptable degree of graphitization. Additionally, the existence of numerous amino and amide groups on the surface is the basic reason for their excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility. The proposed FCDs can emit blue fluorescence under the excitation of ultraviolet light, and disclose an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior with a fluorescence lifetime of 6.43 ns. In the light of the preliminary results of imaging C2C12 cells, a potential application of FCDs has emerged. Along these lines, in vivo bioimaging experiments have also been successfully demonstrated by using FCDs, which implies that the material has clinical application potential.

Xing Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ying Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yueting Wu: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Li Li: Conceptualization, Resources, Project administration and Supervision. Yufei Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Tat Thang Nguyen: Visualization and Software. Chunhua Du: Resources, Project administration and Supervision. Tongxi Zhou: Data curation. Yaru Wei: Data curation. Wei Du: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Talent Introduction Scientific Research Project of Harbin Sport University (RC20-202109), Post-doctoral Mobile Research Station of Harbin Sport University, Disciplinary Echelon Construction Special Project of Harbin Sport University—Echelon Construction Project (XKB10), Disciplinary Echelon Construction Special Project of Harbin Sport University—Leader Project (XKL10), 2021 Basic Scientific Research Business Expense Project of Provincial Undergraduate Universities in Heilongjiang Province (2021KYYWF-PY04).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physe.2022.115200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from banana peel waste using hydrothermal process for in vivo bioimaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots/hydrogel nanocomposite with stable Fe3+ sensing capability

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted synthesis of wavelength-tunable photoluminescent carbon nanodots and their potential applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Yellow-emissive carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for chromium(VI)
